1.1.name: Socratic method, guided discovery, and direct work

deductions.reason: The tutor never explains its pedagogical approach or limitations. It consistently uses Socratic questioning but does not tell the student why it is structuring the conversation this way or clarify any policy constraints. The student asks for a walkthrough in turn 1 and the tutor redirects to questions without explaining the learning rationale for doing so.

The tutor moves the student through cases and toward a principle but never pauses to ask the student to reflect on their own thinking process — e.g., what changed in their reasoning between switch and push, or why their approach to Blood felt different from earlier cases. Transitions between cases are content-driven, not reflection-driven.

overview: The tutor demonstrates strong Socratic technique throughout, consistently using targeted questions to guide the student through trolley cases, act consequentialism, and toward formulating their own general principle. The student progresses from 'honestly kind of lost' to articulating a coherent side-constraint position with a draft essay outline.

Scaffolding is well-executed: the tutor builds case-by-case (switch -> footbridge -> Blood -> general principle -> edge cases), acknowledges correct student work, and adjusts questioning based on the student's current understanding.

The main weaknesses are in meta-learning: the tutor never explains why it uses the question-driven approach (especially when the student explicitly asks for a walkthrough in turn 1), and it does not prompt the student to reflect on shifts in their own thinking process across cases.
Dialogue quality is excellent with clear progression and no redundancy. Communication is clear, well-structured with numbered questions, and maintains an encouraging tone throughout.
